root.title: Cúram  8.1.2
root.summary: This node is the full Cúram Appeals Guide for version 8.1.2. It introduces the appeals and legal actions module, covering appeal concepts and process stages (hearing, hearing review, judicial review), appellants/respondents, multiple appeals, legal petitions, hearings, orders, user/participant roles, lifecycle flows, deadlines, correspondence, data maintenance, and administration. It also includes configurable appeal setup topics such as security, process levels/types, time constraints, clauses, legal action types/categories, and legal statuses. The section is largely navigational and reference-oriented, with a detailed table of contents and end matter, plus legal notices, privacy policy, and trademarks.

children.title: Note
children.summary: Introductory note stating that, before using the information or the supported product, the reader should review the “Notices” section on page 55. Includes Merative US L.P. copyright information for 2012 and 2024.

children.title: Edition
Edition — pages 5-14
children.summary: Introductory overview for the Appeals module. It states the edition applies to Cúram 8.1, 8.1.1, and 8.1.2, then begins the table of contents. The section introduces appeal business concepts: appeal types (hearing, hearing review, judicial review), appellants/respondent, multiple decisions, appeal levels, legal actions (petitions, hearings, orders), user and participant roles, lifecycle, deadlines, correspondence, maintenance, and administration. It also explains that appeals and legal actions map to organizational business requirements.

children.title: Edition — pages 15-24
children.summary: Overview of appeals processing in Cúram, focusing on roles, participants, lifecycles, decisions, and statuses for hearing appeals, hearing reviews, and judicial reviews. It defines user responsibilities such as hearing official, scheduler, worker, case owner, reviewer, and senior reviewer; explains appellants, respondents, representatives, interpreters, and witnesses; and covers online appeal requests, hearing scheduling, adjournment/continuance, decision documents, approval/implementation, cancellation/reopening, and status tables for appeals, hearings, and decisions.

children.title: Edition — pages 25-34
children.summary: Covers the appeals business overview for hearing review appeals and judicial review appeals, including lifecycle stages from creation, approval, hearing scheduling/completion, decision entry, document attachment, approval, implementation, cancellation, and closure. Explains statuses, notices, rescheduling/continuance/adjournment, and how accepted/remanded/rejected/not decided resolutions affect implementation. Also introduces legal action process overview, with example and core concepts for legal petitions and legal hearings, including participants, tracking, scheduling, outcomes, and possible legal orders.

children.title: Edition — pages 35-44
children.summary: This section covers the appeals business overview for legal orders, appeal deadlines, and appeal correspondence. It explains what legal orders are, how legal actions can be associated, and how deadlines are calculated and escalated for filing appeals, reaching and implementing decisions, scheduling hearings, sending correspondence, and submitting statements. It also details correspondence generated during appeals, including receipt notices, hearing notices, adjournment/continuance notices, attendance cancellations, representative fee notices, statement mailings, witness notices/summonses, and decision/order letters.

children.title: Edition — pages 45-54
children.summary: Covers appeals business overview and maintenance details: appeal, hearing, review, judicial review, and cancellation correspondence; decision resolutions, overall decisions, appeal deadlines, court petitions, hearing transcripts. Describes search functions, issues under appeal, appellants, transcription requests, statements, phone contacts, appeal relationships, and auto-created appeal events. Also explains appeal security rights/SIDs, configurable appeal process levels and types, plus legal action administration for clauses, legal action types/categories, and legal statuses. Ends with related guide references.

Introductory overview of the Cúram Appeals module for versions 8.1, 8.1.1, and 8.1.2. Covers appeal concepts and processes: hearing, hearing review, and judicial review; appellants/respondents and multiple appellants; legal petitions, hearings, and orders; user/participant roles; lifecycle, deadlines, correspondence, maintenance, and administration. Also includes appeal security, configurable appeal process levels/types, legal action type/category setup, legal statuses, and related guide references.

children.title: Notices
Privacy policy
children.summary: This page contains legal notices: fictitious-name disclaimer for examples, copyright license terms for sample programs, warranty/liability disclaimer, a link to Merative’s privacy policy, trademark notices for Merative and third-party brands (IBM, Adobe, Oracle, Linux, Microsoft, UNIX), and the copyright notice © Merative US L.P. 2012, 2024.

children.title: Trademarks
children.summary: Lists trademark and copyright notices for Merative and third-party brands, plus a privacy policy link. Identifies Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft/Windows, and UNIX as trademarks or registered trademarks, and notes other names may belong to others. Also includes a copyright license for sample source programs, allowing copy/modify/distribute under certain conditions, with “AS IS” warranty disclaimer and liability limits.

Legal notices and usage terms for the publication: personal use is allowed with proprietary notices preserved; enterprise-only commercial use is allowed under similar limits; derivative works and external distribution require Merative consent. It also includes rights restrictions, export-law compliance, AS-IS/no-warranty and liability disclaimers, patent notice, non-Merative product/site disclaimers, and the copyright notice © Merative US L.P. 2012, 2024.

children.title: Contents
children.summary: Table of contents for the appeals and legal actions documentation. Lists major sections covering appeals business overview, legal actions, user/participant roles, lifecycle flows, process overviews, deadlines, correspondence templates, maintenance of appeal/legal action data, administration, and additional information. Includes page references for subtopics such as appeal types, hearing and judicial review lifecycles, petitions/hearings/orders, notices, statements, transcripts, security, and legal statuses. Useful as a navigation index rather than substantive content.

children.title: 1 Appeals business overview
1.1 Appeals
Appellants and Respondent
children.summary: Defines appellants and respondents in Cúram Appeals. An appellant is the party, usually the primary applicant, who disputes an organization’s decision and submits an appeal; an appeal case always has at least one appellant and may include multiple appellants for related decisions. A respondent defends the original decision. The organization is always one of the two parties: if it is respondent, the appellant is usually the primary client or nominee; if respondent is a case participant, the appellant must be the organization. Also notes that unrelated interested participants may be added as appellants.

children.title: Third Parties
children.summary: Defines third parties in hearing appeals as participants other than appellant and respondent (e.g., employer, information provider, service supplier). Explains that third parties may be added or removed, are not required to attend hearings, and are notified of outcomes or removal from scheduled hearings. Also covers appeals against multiple decisions: one appeal can include multiple items and participants, combined at the organization’s discretion, with resolutions entered per item. Introduces appeal types and states that hearing appeals manage hearings overseen by an uninvolved hearing official.

children.title: Appeals Against Multiple Decisions
children.summary: This section explains how a single appeal case can cover multiple decisions or items under appeal, including cases involving different participants. It notes that the organization may combine related appeals into one hearing, with separate resolutions entered for each item and an overall decision derived from them. It also introduces appeal types and begins describing the hearing appeal type, where hearings are used to review decisions and are overseen by an uninvolved hearing official.

children.title: Appeal types
Hearing Appeal Type
children.summary: Defines the Hearing Appeal type: a hearing appeal manages hearings to challenge a decision on an item under appeal. Hearings are scheduled to resolve appellants’ decisions and are conducted by the organization, overseen by a hearing official not involved in the original decision. The section also covers third parties in hearing appeals (e.g., employers or other participants), their optional attendance, notification if absent or removed, and how one appeal can combine multiple decisions or items under appeal.

children.title: Hearing Review Appeal Type
children.summary: This section explains the hearing review appeal type and how it differs from a hearing appeal. A hearing review is an appeals council review handled by one or more impartial hearing reviewers, not a hearing official, and they decide the issues and record the decision. Unlike hearing appeals, only representatives are invited to attend hearing reviews. The section also contrasts hearing reviews with judicial reviews, which are court-based, outside organizational control, and only the existence of the review is entered in the system.

children.title: Judicial Review Appeal Type
children.summary: This section explains judicial review appeal type: a court conducts the appeal to overturn an item decision, and the court has jurisdiction to overturn it. Unlike other appeal types, it occurs outside the organization, so the organization does not manage the decision-making. Only the fact that the review is taking place is entered in the system; the final decision is later recorded by an organization user. A judicial review may be requested by a participant or the organization and begins with a court petition attached to the case.

Defines the three appeal types in Cúram: hearing appeal, hearing review appeal, and judicial review. Hearing appeals are organization-run hearings overseen by a hearing official, with optional third parties and support for combining multiple decisions/items under one appeal. Hearing reviews are impartial appeals council reviews led by hearing reviewers; only representatives attend. Judicial reviews are court-based, outside organizational control, and the system records only that the review exists until the final decision is entered later.

children.title: Appeal Levels
children.summary: Explains appeal levels in Cúram: an appeal process can have one or more configurable stages, each assigned an appeal type. Organizations may use any combination of hearing appeal, hearing review, and judicial review, and can reuse the same type across multiple levels. Escalation to the next stage is configured per item under appeal in Administration, and later levels can consider new evidence linked to the earlier appeal. Also introduces legal actions, including legal petition, legal hearing, and legal order, with petitions leading to court action.

Overview of Cúram appeals: an appeal is a request to overturn a decision and may progress through configurable stages in an appeal process. It explains appellants, respondents, and optional third parties, including who can be added and how multiple appellants/items can be combined into one hearing with separate resolutions. It also introduces the three appeal types—hearing appeal, hearing review, and judicial review—highlighting that hearings are organization-run, reviews are by impartial hearing reviewers, and judicial reviews are court-based and mostly outside system control.

children.title: 1.2 Legal Actions
Legal Petitions
children.summary: Defines legal petitions as a type of legal action: a formal request to a court involving a participant, such as filing to remove a child from unsafe conditions. Explains roles of petitioner (requesting party, often an organization representative) and respondent (subject of the petition). Notes that petitions typically lead to a formal or informal court hearing and are part of a broader legal actions framework alongside hearings and orders.

children.title: Legal Hearings
children.summary: Defines legal hearings as proceedings before a judge or legal authority (court, tribunal, administrative law judge) that can arise from petitions or legal orders. Uses an example where a petition leads to a scheduled hearing, a witness is called, and a judge finds the respondent guilty and orders 100 hours of community service. Also notes that hearings can create legal orders, which record judgments or sentencing details and may be created from hearings, petitions, or independently.

children.title: Legal Orders
children.summary: Explains legal orders as directives from a legal authority concerning a participant, usually for or against them. Orders may arise from a legal hearing, a legal petition, or independently, and record judgment details such as sentences or directions to participants. Example: a 100-hour community service sentence is recorded as a detention order. Notes that legal order actions do not distinguish participant roles like petitioner or respondent.

Defines legal actions as organization-managed directives or activities involving participants under a legal authority. Introduces three main types: legal petitions, legal hearings, and legal orders. Petitions are formal court requests with petitioner and respondent roles and often lead to hearings. Hearings are proceedings before a judge or tribunal and can result from petitions or orders; they may produce judgments or sentences. Orders are directives from a legal authority, created from hearings, petitions, or independently, and record judgment details without distinguishing petitioner/respondent roles.

children.title: 1.3 Appeal Users and Participants
Hearing Case Users
Hearing Case Owner
children.summary: Defines the hearing case owner role in an appeal hearing. The owner is the user who creates the hearing case and, if no hearing worker is assigned, maintains hearing details such as statements, transcription requests, and events. This section also notes the relationship to other hearing roles, especially that the hearing case supervisor approves the case and hearing workers normally manage hearings.

children.title: Hearing Case Supervisor
children.summary: Defines the Hearing Case Supervisor role in appeal hearing cases. The supervisor approves or rejects creation of a hearing case and must approve it before a hearing can be scheduled. This role typically defaults to the user the hearing case owner reports to. The section sits within an overview of appeal users and participants, alongside other hearing-related user roles.

children.title: Hearing Scheduler
children.summary: Defines appeal users and participants for hearing, hearing review, and judicial review cases. It explains the main hearing case roles—owner, supervisor, scheduler, worker, case owner, hearing official, and senior hearing official—and what each does. The hearing scheduler is responsible for scheduling hearings after a hearing case is approved; the system issues a task to all users with that role, and the first to reserve it handles scheduling. Also notes related responsibilities for hearing case owners and supervisors.

children.title: Hearing Worker
children.summary: Defines the Hearing Worker role in appeal hearing cases. The hearing worker manages hearings, including scheduling/rescheduling and recording attendance. If no hearing worker is assigned, the hearing case owner performs these tasks. After a hearing is scheduled, the system creates a task for available users with hearing worker privileges; the user who reserves it becomes the assigned hearing worker.

children.title: Case Owner
children.summary: Explains the appeal user roles involved in hearing cases and defines the “Case Owner” role. The case owner is the owner of the item under appeal, is automatically included in the hearing case, and is assigned a user role. This user is responsible for implementing hearing case decisions and may be invited to attend hearings. Also situates the role among other hearing users such as hearing case owner, supervisor, scheduler, and worker.

children.title: Hearing Official and Senior Hearing Official
children.summary: Defines the hearing official’s role in a hearing appeal: presiding over the hearing, deciding the appeal, creating decision documentation, and supervising hearing case management. Also outlines hearing case participants: appellants, respondents, representatives, third parties, interpreters, and witnesses, including fee approval for representatives and summons/correspondence rules for witnesses. Introduces hearing review users and roles, and describes the hearing review owner as the creator and manager of hearing review details when no hearing worker is assigned.

Overview of appeal hearing users and participant roles. It defines seven hearing case user roles: hearing case owner, supervisor, scheduler, worker, case owner, hearing official, and senior hearing official. The owner creates and maintains hearing details if no worker is assigned; the supervisor approves the case so scheduling can begin; the scheduler is assigned a task to schedule hearings; and the worker manages scheduling/rescheduling and attendance. The case owner is the appellant/item owner, automatically included and responsible for implementing decisions.

children.title: Hearing Case Participants
Representatives
children.summary: Explains the role of representatives in hearing appeals: they attend hearings on behalf of one or more appellants, can be added or removed from appellant associations, and any fee they charge must be approved by the organization before payment. The section also situates representatives among hearing case participants and briefly notes related participant types like interpreters and witnesses, plus hearing review user roles and the hearing review owner’s responsibilities.

children.title: Interpreters
children.summary: Describes the role of interpreters in hearing appeals: they may be needed to help attendees communicate at hearings, can be registered service suppliers, and unregistered interpreters are registered as representative participants in the system. This section sits among other hearing case participant roles such as appellants, respondents, representatives, third parties, and witnesses.

children.title: Witnesses
children.summary: Witnesses in hearing appeals are people who testify under oath and may be called on behalf of all appellants. If a witness is not voluntary, a summons is issued; otherwise, hearing schedule correspondence is sent. Examples include doctors and public health nurses. A witness may be a registered person or an unregistered participant later registered as a representative for the appeal.

This section defines hearing case participants in a hearing appeal: appellants, respondent, representatives, third parties, interpreters, and witnesses. It explains that representatives attend for appellants, can be linked/unlinked to appellants, and any fee requires organization approval. Interpreters may assist communication and unregistered interpreters are registered as representative participants. Witnesses testify under oath, may be summoned if unwilling, and receive hearing schedule correspondence otherwise.

children.title: Hearing Review Users
Hearing Review Owner
children.summary: Defines the Hearing Review Owner role. The hearing review owner is the caseworker who creates the initial hearing review/appeal request and maintains key hearing review information such as statements, transcription requests, and events. They also manage hearing details for the case when no hearing worker is assigned. The surrounding page also introduces hearing officials and participant roles, but this section specifically focuses on owner responsibilities in the hearing review lifecycle.

children.title: Hearing Review Supervisor
children.summary: Defines hearing review roles and responsibilities. The hearing review supervisor approves or rejects creation and typically defaults to the person the owner reports to. It explains the hearing scheduler role for assigning hearings after approval, the hearing worker role for managing hearing details, and the case owner’s role in implementing decisions. It also covers hearing reviewers, participants/representatives, fee approval for paid representatives, and notes that judicial review has only limited user and participant roles because it is handled outside the organization.

children.title: Hearing Scheduler for the Hearing Review
children.summary: Defines the hearing review scheduler role and related hearing review roles. The hearing scheduler is the user who reserves the task to schedule hearings after a review is approved; the system issues this task to available users with the hearing scheduler role. The section also explains nearby roles: hearing review supervisor, hearing worker, case owner, reviewers, and participants, including their responsibilities in managing, attending, and deciding hearings.

children.title: Hearing Worker for the Hearing Review
children.summary: Describes hearing review roles and responsibilities. A hearing worker may be assigned to manage hearings, including attendance and attachments; if none is assigned, the case owner handles hearing details. After scheduling, the system creates a task for a user with hearing worker privileges, who becomes the assigned hearing worker when the task is reserved. This section also notes the hearing review supervisor, scheduler, case owner, reviewers, participants, and judicial review users.

children.title: Case Owner
children.summary: Defines the case owner’s role in a hearing review. The case owner of the appealed case or issue is automatically included as a hearing review user role, is responsible for implementing hearing review decisions, and may be invited to hearings. The page also situates this role among other hearing review roles such as supervisor, scheduler, hearing worker, reviewers, participants, and judicial review users.

children.title: Hearing Reviewers
children.summary: Defines hearing review roles and responsibilities. Covers the hearing review supervisor (approves/rejects review creation and defaults to the owner’s manager), the hearing scheduler (reserves the hearing-scheduling task after approval), the hearing worker (maintains hearing details like attendance and attachments), the case owner (included automatically and implements decisions), and the hearing reviewer (presides over the hearing, decides the review, and creates decision documentation). Also notes hearing review participants and representatives.

Introduces hearing review user roles in the appeal lifecycle. Lists seven roles: hearing review owner, supervisor, review hearing scheduler, review hearing worker, case owner, hearing reviewer, and senior hearing reviewer. Defines the hearing review owner as the caseworker who creates the initial hearing review/appeal request, maintains statements, transcription requests, and events, and manages hearing details when no hearing worker is assigned. Also frames the page with related hearing official and participant roles.

children.title: Hearing Review Participants
children.summary: Defines the roles involved in a hearing review: supervisor, hearing scheduler, hearing worker, case owner, reviewers, and participants. It explains who approves the review, who schedules hearings, who maintains hearing details, and how tasks are assigned when a review is approved or a hearing is scheduled. It also notes that participants include appellants, respondents, and representatives, and that representative fees require organization approval.

children.title: Judicial Review Users and Case Participants
children.summary: Explains roles in a hearing/judicial review process: hearing review supervisor approves creation; hearing scheduler schedules hearings; hearing worker manages hearing details if assigned; case owner is automatically included and implements decisions; hearing reviewers preside and issue decisions; hearing review participants include appellants, respondents, and representatives. For judicial review specifically, only a few roles exist because it is handled outside the organization: the judicial review case owner and the case owner of the original appealed items.

Overview of user and participant roles in appeal cases for hearing, hearing review, and judicial review. It defines hearing/hearing review lifecycle roles: owner, supervisor, scheduler, worker, case owner, and decision-maker (hearing official/reviewer), including tasks for creating cases, approving them, scheduling hearings, and managing attendance/details. It also covers participants such as appellants, respondents, representatives, third parties, interpreters, and witnesses, including fee approval for representatives and summons/correspondence for witnesses. Judicial review is mostly external, with only two roles.

children.title: 1.4 Appeal Lifecycle
Online Appeal Requests
Online Appeal Requests Lifecycle
children.summary: This section describes the lifecycle of online appeal requests in Cúram. It covers how eligible citizens submit appeal requests online via an IEG script, how the request is stored on the citizen account, and how a record and PDF are created on the primary appellant’s person record. It also explains workflow task generation for appeals workers, linking the request to a created appeal for traceability, and citizen communications when a request leads to an appeal.

Cúram lets eligible citizens submit appeal requests online as part of the appeal lifecycle. Using a configurable IEG script, the citizen provides appellant, item, and reason details; the request is shown on their citizen account with a processing status. An appeal request record and PDF are created on the primary appellant’s person record, and a workflow task is sent to appeals staff to review and create an appeal. The request can be linked to the appeal for traceability, and citizen account messages are sent when it leads to an appeal.

children.title: Hearing Appeal Type Lifecycle
Creating a Hearing Appeal Type
children.summary: Explains the hearing appeal lifecycle and how a hearing appeal type is created. Covers creation of the hearing case, defining the item under appeal, appellant/respondent, and appeal details. Notes that multiple items can be added, possibly with different appellants, and each must be approved before a hearing is scheduled. Describes how hearing, decision, and case lifecycles merge, how decisions are entered and approved or rejected, and that hearing appeals can be canceled and reopened if good reason is shown.

children.title: Approving a Hearing Appeal Type
children.summary: This section explains the approval stage for a hearing appeal type. It states that hearing appeals can only be created for issue cases with approved resolutions, and that each appealed item linked to the hearing case must be approved or rejected, typically by a hearing case supervisor. Approval determines whether the hearing can proceed; if multiple items are under appeal, all must be approved for the case to be fully approved. Issues added to the hearing appeal do not require approval. It also introduces the hearing lifecycle that follows approval.

children.title: Hearing Lifecycle
Scheduling a Hearing for a Hearing Appeal Type
children.summary: Explains the first hearing-lifecycle stage for hearing appeal cases: scheduling the hearing. It distinguishes hearing types available when there is one appellant—telephone, home, location, and desk-based hearings—and notes that desk-based hearings are decided from statements and evidence only. This section is the start of the process after appeal approval and is relevant for understanding how hearing format is selected.

children.title: Completing a Hearing for a Hearing Appeal Type
children.summary: This section explains the final hearing lifecycle steps for hearing appeal cases, focusing on completing a hearing. It describes how the hearing official marks a hearing complete, records attendee attendance, and how absent attendees may allow the case to be reopened later; attendance also affects representative payment. It also briefly covers rescheduling hearings for organizational conflicts and continuing hearings before the scheduled date, including notice requirements and copied participant information.

children.title: Rescheduling a Hearing for a Hearing Appeal Type
children.summary: This section explains how a hearing appeal hearing is rescheduled. Rescheduling is used for organization-side scheduling conflicts such as date, time, or location issues, and cannot be requested by case participants (they must request a continuance instead). When rescheduled, participant and user details, and any transcription requests, are copied to the new hearing. Required participants are notified if there is enough time for correspondence, desk-based hearings create a task for the hearing official, and the new statement submission date is communicated.

children.title: Continuing a Hearing for a Hearing Appeal Type
children.summary: Explains hearing continuance for hearing appeals: a scheduled hearing may be postponed before its date, such as when a participant is unavailable. Continued hearings require a new hearing to be scheduled before the case can advance. When continued, notices are sent to all case participants about the postponed hearing.

children.title: Adjourning a Hearing for a Hearing Appeal Type
children.summary: Explains how hearing adjournments work for hearing appeal cases: a hearing may be postponed while in session, the hearing official adjourns it, and the scheduler records the reason and attendance, schedules a new hearing, and sends adjournment notices. Desk-based hearings cannot be adjourned. Also outlines the hearing appeal decision lifecycle after a hearing ends: the official enters resolutions for each item, may attach a decision document, and the system derives the overall decision, which must then be approved or rejected before implementation.

Covers the hearing appeal lifecycle after approval: scheduling, completing, rescheduling, and continuing hearings. Scheduling options depend on appellant count: one appellant can have telephone, home, location, or desk-based hearings; multiple appellants allow only location or desk-based. Scheduled hearings notify required participants and may invite users; desk-based hearings rely only on statements/evidence and have no transcriptions. Completion records attendance and can enable reopening and representative payment. Rescheduling is organization-initiated; continuance is participant-driven before the hearing date.

children.title: Hearing Appeal Type Decision Lifecycle
Entering a Hearing Appeal Type Decision
children.summary: Explains how to enter a hearing appeal type decision after a hearing is completed. The hearing official records a resolution for each item under appeal, using accepted, remanded, not decided, or rejected. A decision document may be attached or created to describe the issues and outcomes. The system combines item resolutions to determine the overall hearing appeal case decision, which confirms whether all items have been resolved and ends the first stage of the hearing appeal decision lifecycle.

children.title: Attaching a Decision Document
children.summary: Explains how to attach or create a hearing appeal decision document. Supports external documents via reference/location or attachment, or internal Word-template-based documents with auto-filled case data and reusable appeal clauses. Also covers decision approval workflow: hearing official submits for senior approval (or auto-approval via security/role), with statuses submitted/approved/open and closure after approval or rejection. Finally describes implementing accepted decisions through tasks for case owners to override evidence, eligibility, or payments, and notes remanded/rejected/not decided outcomes don’t require overrides.

children.title: Approving a Hearing Appeal Type Decision
children.summary: Explains how hearing appeal type decisions are approved and implemented. A hearing official submits the decision for approval; a senior hearing official approves or rejects it, unless automatic approval applies via security rights or submission by the senior official. Status changes from submitted to approved, or back to open if rejected, and the appeal deadline task is completed when decided. Also covers creation/attachment of decision documents and implementation of accepted resolutions by case owners, including overrides for evidence, eligibility, or payment details.

children.title: Implementing Accepted Decisions
children.summary: Describes the final hearing appeal lifecycle step: implementing accepted decisions. The system creates a task for each appealed item and assigns it to the case owner to override the original decision by updating evidence, eligibility, or payment details. Rejected, remanded, or not decided resolutions do not require overrides; remanded decisions trigger notification to create a new appeal case. The process ends once the decision resolution is implemented.

Covers the hearing appeal decision lifecycle after a hearing: entering item-level resolutions (accepted, remanded, not decided, rejected), creating/attaching decision documents, and how the system derives the overall case decision. Explains approval workflow (hearing official submits, senior hearing official approves/rejects or auto-approval via security), status changes, and closure of the appeal deadline task. Also describes implementing accepted decisions through case-owner tasks to override evidence, eligibility, or payment details; remanded outcomes trigger a new appeal case.

children.title: Canceling a Hearing Appeal Type
children.summary: Explains when a hearing appeal case can be canceled or reopened, and outlines status tracking for hearing appeal cases. Cancellation can happen at any processing stage; if a hearing is already scheduled, it is also canceled and participants are notified. Reopening is allowed only for a closed appeal when required attendees missed the last hearing for good reason. Also introduces the three status lifecycles—hearing appeal case status, hearing status, and decision status—and defines major hearing appeal case statuses such as open, active, canceled, approved, delayed process pending, and closed.

children.title: Reopening a Hearing Appeal Type
children.summary: Explains when a closed hearing appeal can be reopened: only if an appellant, respondent, or required third party did not attend the last completed hearing and has good reason for non-attendance; no other reopening cases are allowed. Also notes the related hearing appeal lifecycle context: cancellations can occur at any stage, scheduled hearings are canceled and participants notified, and status history includes Open, Active, Canceled, Approved, Delayed Process Pending, and Closed.

children.title: Hearing Appeal Type Statuses
Hearing Appeal Status History
children.summary: Explains hearing appeal lifecycle controls and status history. Covers canceling a hearing appeal at any stage, including canceling any scheduled hearing and notifying participants, and reopening a closed appeal only when a required attendee missed the last hearing for good reason. Describes that hearing appeal status history tracks case progression, with statuses open, active, canceled, approved, delayed process pending, and closed. Also notes separate status lifecycles exist for the case, individual hearings, and decision statuses.

children.title: Hearing Statuses
children.summary: Explains hearing appeal lifecycle handling. Covers canceling a hearing appeal at any stage, which also cancels any scheduled hearing and notifies participants, and reopening a closed hearing appeal only when required attendees missed the last hearing for good reason. Introduces three status families: hearing appeal case statuses, hearing statuses, and hearing appeal decision statuses. Lists hearing appeal case statuses: Open, Active, Canceled, Approved, Delayed Process Pending, and Closed, with brief definitions and notes that status history is maintained throughout the lifecycle.

children.title: Hearing Appeal Type Decision Statuses
children.summary: Defines hearing appeal decision statuses and their lifecycle. It lists the possible decision states for a hearing case: Not Started, In Progress, Submitted, Approved, and Rejected, with brief meanings for each. It also situates these statuses within the broader hearing review appeal lifecycle, noting that a hearing review must be approved before a hearing can be scheduled, and that the hearing and decision lifecycles merge when a decision is entered.

Describes hearing appeal type lifecycle statuses and related controls. Covers canceling a hearing appeal at any stage, which cancels any scheduled hearing and notifies participants, and reopening a closed appeal only when a required attendee missed the last hearing for good reason. Explains that three status families exist: hearing appeal case statuses, hearing statuses, and hearing appeal decision statuses. Details case status history and the case statuses Open, Active, Canceled, Approved, Delayed Process Pending, and Closed, including when each applies.

Explains the full hearing appeal lifecycle: creating the hearing case, adding items under appeal, approving/rejecting appealed items, scheduling and completing hearings, entering and approving decisions, implementing accepted outcomes, and canceling/reopening cases. Covers hearing options for single vs multiple appellants, notifications, continuance/rescheduling, adjournment, decision documents, and status families for case, hearing, and decision lifecycles. Use this node for end-to-end process details and lifecycle/status behavior.

children.title: Hearing Review Appeal Type Lifecycle
Creating a Hearing Review Appeal Type
children.summary: Explains how to create a hearing review appeal type and the first lifecycle stage. Covers what can be appealed (decisions on items under appeal, including issue cases with approved resolutions), required details (appellant, respondent, difficulty, reason, emergency, continued benefits, dates), and optional receipt/appeal notices. Also describes the approval stage, where each appealed item is approved or rejected to confirm the hearing can be scheduled; multiple items all require approval, while issues added do not.

children.title: Approving a Hearing Review Appeal Type
children.summary: This section explains the approval stage of a hearing review appeal. It verifies whether a hearing can be scheduled by approving or rejecting each appealed item, typically by a hearing review supervisor. If multiple appealed items exist, all must be approved for the hearing to proceed; rejected items keep the appeal open. The hearing review status follows the status of the appealed item, but issue cases added to the appeal do not require approval.

children.title: Hearing Lifecycle for a Hearing Review Appeal Type
Scheduling a Hearing for a Hearing Review
children.summary: Explains the hearing lifecycle for a hearing review, focusing on scheduling, completion, rescheduling, and continuation. A hearing review hearing is presided over by organization hearing reviewers who were not involved in the original decision or prior appeal, and a minimum number of reviewers is required. Scheduling can use a chosen date or automatic search for availability. The section also notes that hearings must be completed before a decision is entered, and describes how rescheduling copies participants and notifications, while continuances postpone the hearing before the scheduled date.

children.title: Completing a Hearing for a Hearing Review
children.summary: Explains the hearing lifecycle for a hearing review after approval: scheduling, completing, rescheduling, and continuing hearings. A hearing review is heard by organization hearing reviewers who had no prior role in the original decision or appeal. Scheduling requires selecting enough available reviewers, either by date lookup or system search. Completion requires a reviewer to mark the hearing complete and record appellant attendance, which can affect representative payment. Rescheduling is for organizational conflicts and copies participants/transcription requests; continuances are requested by participants and must occur before the hearing date.

children.title: Rescheduling a Hearing for a Hearing Review
children.summary: Explains when and why a hearing for a hearing review can be rescheduled. Rescheduling is allowed only by the organization for conflicts in hearing date, time, or location, not at a participant’s request. When rescheduled, the system copies participant and user details and any transcription request to the new hearing, and notifies all participants. This section sits within the hearing lifecycle, alongside scheduling, completion, and continuance.

children.title: Continuing a Hearing for a Hearing Review
children.summary: Explains how a hearing for a hearing review can be continued. A continuance is postponing a scheduled hearing to a later date, such as when a participant cannot attend. It can only be requested before the hearing date. After a continuation, a new hearing must be scheduled before the review can advance. The section sits within the hearing lifecycle and contrasts continuation with rescheduling and adjournment.

children.title: Adjourning a Hearing for a Hearing Review
children.summary: Explains how to adjourn a hearing during a hearing review, including that hearing reviewers postpone the session, the scheduler records the adjournment and attendance, and notices are sent to participants. It then describes the hearing review decision lifecycle after the hearing ends: entering overall decisions based on item-level resolutions, the possible resolution types (accepted, remanded, not decided, rejected), attaching or creating decision documents, and approval/rejection leading to implementation of appeal decisions.

Overview of the hearing review hearing lifecycle from approval through completion. It explains that hearing reviewers must be organization users with no prior role in the original decision or appeal, and that a hearing must be scheduled and completed before a decision can be entered. Scheduling requires enough available reviewers, found by date lookup or automatic search. It also covers completion, where attendance is recorded, plus rescheduling for organizational conflicts and continuances requested before the hearing date.

children.title: Hearing Review Appeal Type Decision Lifecycle
Entering a Hearing Review Decision
children.summary: Explains how a hearing review decision is entered after the hearing ends. The hearing official records an overall decision based on item-level resolutions: accepted, remanded, not decided, or rejected. It notes that accepted items require action, rejected items uphold the original decision, and the system uses the entered resolutions to determine the overall hearing review decision. Also introduces attaching or creating a decision document that records the appealed items and outcomes, possibly multiple documents for multiple decisions.

children.title: Attaching a Decision Document
children.summary: Explains how a decision document is attached to a hearing review decision. The document records the appealed items considered and the decisions reached at the hearing; multiple decision documents may exist if multiple decisions were made. This section is part of the hearing review decision lifecycle, which begins after the hearing, when resolutions are entered and an overall accepted/rejected decision is determined.

children.title: Approving a Hearing Review Decision
children.summary: This section explains how hearing review decisions are approved before implementation. It covers submission by the hearing reviewer, approval or rejection by a senior hearing reviewer, automatic approval when the reviewer has approval rights, and status changes from submitted to approved or back to open if rejected. It also notes that approval/rejection completes the appeal deadline task and closes the hearing review. Related content includes implementing accepted decisions and canceling a hearing review appeal type.

children.title: Implementing Accepted Decisions
children.summary: This section explains how accepted hearing review decisions are implemented. It covers creating decision documents (external references/attachments or Word templates with auto-filled case data and appeal clauses), approving or rejecting hearing review decisions, and the final implementation step where case owners override evidence, eligibility, or payment details to match the appeal outcome. It also notes that rejected, remanded, or not decided resolutions usually require no override, and remanded cases trigger notification to create a new appeal.

Describes the hearing review decision lifecycle after a hearing ends: entering item-level resolutions (accepted, remanded, not decided, rejected), attaching or creating decision documents, and having the system derive the overall accepted/rejected appeal decision from those resolutions. Notes that accepted items require action while rejected items uphold the original decision, and that multiple decision documents may exist if multiple decisions are made.

children.title: Canceling a Hearing Review Appeal Type
children.summary: This section explains that a hearing review appeal can be canceled at any stage, such as when the appellant withdraws the request. It indicates the lifecycle is interruptible and likely covers the cancellation conditions and effects on processing.

children.title: Hearing Review Statuses
Hearing Review Status History
children.summary: Defines the status history lifecycle for hearing reviews and related hearing statuses. Hearing review statuses include Open, Active, Canceled, Approved, Delayed Process Pending, and Closed, with notes on when each applies. It also explains that a hearing review maintains status history throughout its lifecycle and that a hearing review can have associated hearing statuses such as Scheduled and Completed, with only one scheduled hearing allowed at a time.

children.title: Hearing Statuses for a Hearing Review
children.summary: This section defines the status lifecycles for a hearing review and its hearings. It explains that hearing review status history is maintained throughout the lifecycle and lists review statuses: Open, Active, Canceled, Approved, Delayed Process Pending, and Closed, with when each applies. It also describes hearing statuses for a hearing review: Scheduled and Completed, noting only one hearing can be scheduled at a time. Useful for understanding how hearing reviews and hearings progress and when decisions or cancellations occur.

children.title: Hearing Review Decision Statuses
children.summary: Defines hearing review decision lifecycle statuses and when each applies: Not Started (no decision), In Progress (decision exists but not submitted), Submitted (sent for approval), Approved (authorized user approves), and Rejected (authorized user rejects). Also briefly references hearing statuses like Adjourned, Continued, and Canceled, and notes that a continued hearing requires another hearing to proceed. Ends with a short overview of judicial review appeal lifecycle, where processing occurs outside the system and may be canceled at any stage.

Defines hearing review status lifecycles and hearing statuses. Hearing review statuses are: Open (new or reverted after rejection), Active (a hearing is scheduled), Canceled, Approved, Delayed Process Pending (online processing underway), and Closed (decision approved or rejected and system closes the review). It also covers hearing statuses for an individual hearing review: Scheduled and Completed, with only one scheduled hearing allowed at a time. Useful for tracking hearing review progression and status transitions.

Covers the full hearing review appeal lifecycle: creation, approval, hearing scheduling/completion, decision entry and approval, implementation, cancellation, and status tracking. Explains what can be appealed, required appeal details, optional receipt notices, and that issue cases need approved resolutions. Describes reviewer eligibility, scheduling methods, rescheduling/continuance/adjournment rules, decision resolutions and decision documents, and approval workflow. Includes status tables for hearing reviews, hearings, and decision statuses.

children.title: Judicial Review Appeal Type Lifecycle
Creating a Judicial Review and Petitioning the Court
children.summary: Explains how to create a judicial review appeal for denied items or approved-issue cases, add additional appealed items, and specify appellant, respondent, appeal details, and key dates. Notes that the organization may print and attach a court petition, or receive notice when the appellant petitions. Covers the need for a hearing transcription in higher-level reviews and how judicial review decisions are entered: item-level resolutions are set first, then the system auto-derives the overall decision. Includes possible resolutions: accepted, remanded, not decided, rejected.

children.title: Providing a Hearing Transcription
children.summary: Explains judicial review appeal creation and requirements. Covers adding multiple denied items and appellants, limits to issues with approved resolutions, and capturing appellant/respondent, appeal details, dates, and optional court petition generation. Notes that if the organization is not the appellant, the case is created after court notification. A key requirement is providing a hearing transcription from a prior hearing appeal/review appeal, only for judicial reviews beyond first level. Also outlines entering judicial review decisions, automatic approval, and item-level resolutions: accepted, remanded, not decided, rejected.

children.title: Recording the Judicial Review Decision
Attaching a Decision Document
children.summary: Explains how to attach or create a decision document for a judicial review after resolutions are entered. Documents can be external (file reference/location or attached file) or internal (Word template with auto-filled organization, appellant, dates, overall decision, issues, and resolutions). Notes support for appeal clauses and multiple decision documents. Also outlines post-decision implementation: the system notifies owners and creates tasks to enforce accepted decisions, including overriding evidence, eligibility, or payment details; rejected, remanded, or not decided outcomes generally require no override, though remanded cases trigger a new appeal.

Explains how to record a judicial review decision after the hearing. The case owner enters a resolution for each appealed item—accepted, remanded, not decided, or rejected—then the system automatically determines and approves the overall judicial review decision and starts the decision lifecycle. Accepted means the original item must be acted on by the original owner; remanded means reconsideration; not decided means no decision; rejected upholds the original decision. A decision document or documents must also be attached.

children.title: Implementing Judicial Review Decisions
children.summary: Explains how judicial review decisions are implemented after recording the outcome. The system notifies the case owner and creates tasks for owners of appealed items to carry out the decision. For accepted decisions, owners must override evidence, eligibility, or payment details to match the appeal outcome. Rejected, remanded, or not decided decisions usually require no override because the original decision stands, though remanded decisions trigger notice that a new appeal must be created.

children.title: Closing the Judicial Review
children.summary: Explains how a judicial review case is completed, canceled, and tracked. After the decision resolution is implemented, the review must be manually closed in the system by an authorized user, which marks the status as Closed and sends a task to implement the decision. A review may be canceled at any stage, such as when an appellant withdraws, triggering a notification to the owner. Also defines lifecycle statuses: Open, Canceled, Delayed Process Pending, and Closed.

children.title: Canceling a Judicial Review
children.summary: Explains that a judicial review can be canceled at any point in its lifecycle, such as when an appellant withdraws the appeal request. Cancellation triggers a notification task to the judicial review owner. Also notes the related status values for judicial review lifecycle management: Open, Canceled, Delayed Process Pending, and Closed.

children.title: Judicial Review Status
children.summary: Explains judicial review lifecycle status handling. Covers closure after the decision is implemented, manual closing of the case when the decision is recorded, and cancellation at any stage with a notification task to the owner. Defines the four statuses: Open (newly created), Canceled (canceled), Delayed Process Pending (system is running an online process), and Closed (authorized user manually closes the case and sends an implementation task).

Explains the judicial review appeal lifecycle. Covers creating a review for denied items or approved-issue cases, adding multiple items/appellants, entering appellant/respondent details, dates, and optional court petition generation. Notes that a hearing transcription is required for higher-level reviews. Judicial review decisions are recorded with item-level resolutions first; the system auto-derives the overall outcome and auto-approves it. Describes decision document attachment, implementation by item owners, and closure/cancellation behavior. Statuses include Open, Canceled, Delayed Process Pending, and Closed.

Appeal lifecycle overview for all appeal types. Covers appeal requests via hardcopy, phone, email, or online; online request submission, worker review, appeal creation, request-to-appeal traceability, citizen notifications, and request statuses. Describes hearing appeal lifecycle: case creation, adding/approving items, scheduling options, hearing conduct, decisions, implementation, cancel/reopen, and status behavior. Summarizes hearing review lifecycle and status tables, including rescheduling/adjournment rules and approval workflow. Also explains judicial review lifecycle: creation, court petition/transcript, item-level decisions, auto-approved overall outcome, decision documents, implementation, and closure/cancellation.

children.title: 1.5 Legal Action Process Overview
Example Legal Action Process
children.summary: Introduces the legal action process at a high level: legal actions are managed at the case level and typically involve requesting a hearing through a legal petition, conducting the hearing, and making a determination that may result in a legal order. It also begins an example scenario involving an abandoned child case to illustrate how the process works in practice. This section is useful for understanding the workflow and when legal action management applies.

children.title: Legal Petition
children.summary: Explains legal petitions as formal court requests and how Cúram tracks them as legal actions. Covers petition categories/types, key data fields (petition number, court, judge, petitioner, respondent, dates, narrative, outcome), and roles of petitioner/respondent. Notes petitions can be created, modified, closed, and linked to other legal actions. Also introduces legal hearings: their purpose, tracked details, scheduling, notices, managing representatives/witnesses, rescheduling/canceling/adjournment, finalization, and possible outcomes leading to orders or new petitions.

children.title: Legal Hearing
children.summary: Explains the Legal Hearing legal action in Cúram. A hearing is a legal proceeding before a judge or legal authority, created and tracked by selecting category “Hearing” and a type such as Pre-Detention or Detention. Recorded details include docket number, court location, judge, participant, start date, and outcome. The section covers scheduling, notices to participants, adding representatives and witnesses, and managing rescheduled, canceled, adjourned, and finalized hearings. Decisions may close the hearing or lead to a legal order or new petition.

children.title: Legal Order
children.summary: Explains legal orders: court directives about a participant, created from hearings, petitions, or independently under a legal action such as an Order or Detention Order. Covers common order types, what details are recorded (court location, judge, participant, dates, outcome), and that orders can be created, modified, closed, and linked to other legal actions. Also notes legal action associations can be added or removed, and briefly introduces appeal deadlines as configurable, task-escalated deadlines for appeals processing.

children.title: Legal Action Associations
children.summary: Explains how legal actions can be linked to one another, including when a legal action is created within another and the system auto-creates the association. Associations may also be created for other reasons, and incorrect links can be removed. This section helps users understand relationship tracking between related legal actions, especially within legal order workflows.

Overview of legal action management at the case level: petitions request a court ruling, hearings are conducted and decided, and decisions may create legal orders. Includes an example abandoned-child custody workflow showing associated legal actions. Describes petition tracking fields, roles of petitioner/respondent, and that petitions can be created, modified, closed, and linked to other actions. Also covers hearings as court proceedings with scheduling, notices, representatives/witnesses, rescheduling/canceling/adjournment, finalization, and outcomes that may close the hearing or lead to an order or new petition.

children.title: 1.6 Appeal Deadlines
Deadline for Appealing Original Decision
children.summary: This section explains three appeal deadlines: the time limit for appealing an original decision, the deadline for reaching a decision on hearing/hearing review appeals, and the deadline for implementing approved appeal decisions. It describes how deadlines are calculated from appeal receipt and decision dates, how the earliest related item can set the overall appeal decision deadline, and what happens when deadlines are missed, including task escalation, reprioritization, and reassignment.

children.title: Deadline for Reaching a Decision on an Appeal
children.summary: This section explains appeal timing rules. It covers how an appeal is judged timely based on the gap between the original decision date and the appeal request receipt date, and notes that organizations define how untimely appeals are handled. It also describes deadlines for reaching a decision on hearing and hearing review appeals, including how the earliest related item deadline sets the overall case deadline, how new items can update it after approval, and how overdue tasks are escalated. It also covers implementation deadlines for approved appeal decisions.

children.title: Deadline for Implementing a Decision
Extending the Deadline for Reaching a Decision
children.summary: This section explains how appeal decision deadlines can change when a hearing is continued, adjourned, or rescheduled: the system extends the deadline by the delay between the old and new hearing dates. It also notes related timing rules for scheduling hearings, sending correspondence, and submitting statements, all based on time constraints tied to the appeal process. It is useful for understanding deadline calculations and escalation when hearing scheduling is late.

Explains how the system calculates the deadline for implementing an approved appeal decision. The implementation period starts when the appeal request is received, and the deadline is computed by adding the receipt date to the time allowed to reach and implement the decision for each related case. When decisions are approved, implementation deadline tasks are created and assigned to the item owner. If implementation is late, the task is escalated by increasing priority and reassigning it to a supervisor or manager.

children.title: Deadline for Scheduling a Hearing
children.summary: Explains how the schedule hearing deadline is calculated from the appeal decision deadline and hearing-stage time constraints, ensuring enough time to hold the hearing and issue a decision. Describes an interim task for hearing schedulers, how the deadline updates if the overall decision date changes, and escalation if no hearing is scheduled in time. Also notes related timing rules for correspondence and statement submission, which depend on the scheduled hearing date.

children.title: Deadline for Sending Correspondence
children.summary: Explains the appeal case correspondence deadline, which determines whether there is enough time before a scheduled hearing to send correspondence. It is based on a time constraint specifying the minimum required lead time before the hearing. The system compares the current date to the hearing date, subtracts the constraint, and if the result is zero or greater, the correspondence is sent.

children.title: Deadline for Submitting Statements
children.summary: This section explains the hearing statement deadline for appeals. A submitted appeal statement is only considered at the hearing if copies were sent to the relevant case participants in time. The deadline exists to ensure participants have enough time to receive and review the statement before the scheduled hearing.

children.title: Deadline Extension for Reopened Hearing Case
children.summary: Explains how reopened hearing cases handle appeal decision deadlines: the original deadline from the closed hearing case is not replaced, but extended by the time elapsed between case closure and reopening, and a new task is created for the updated deadline. The section also briefly covers hearing statement timing rules and begins the appeal correspondence overview, including appeal request receipt notices and what information they contain.

Overview of appeal deadline management. Covers timeliness of appealing an original decision, deadlines for reaching appeal decisions and implementing approved decisions, and how these are driven by configurable time constraints. Explains deadline calculation from receipt/decision dates, use of the earliest related item to set overall appeal deadlines, and escalation when tasks are overdue. Also covers extending decision deadlines when hearings are continued or rescheduled, plus interim deadlines for scheduling hearings, sending correspondence, and submitting statements.

children.title: 1.7 Appeal Correspondence
Appeal Request Receipt Notice
children.summary: Explains the Appeal Request Receipt Notice generated for appeal cases. It confirms the organization has received an appeal request and created an appeal case, and can be produced as a single or multiple receipt notice when additional cases are added. The notice is sent only to appellants who are case participants. It includes appellant details, appeal case reference number, and issues to be addressed, such as the reason for appeal, receipt date, effective date of the decision appealed, and any requested benefit amount for product delivery/issue cases.

children.title: Appeal Request Rejection Correspondence
children.summary: Explains the automatically generated “Appeal Request Rejection Correspondence” sent when a hearing appeal or hearing review appeal request is rejected, only if the appellant is a case participant. It includes appellant name and address, appeal case reference, rejected issues, rejection notice, issue date, final date to submit a written request for a new appeal, and organization/contact details plus the current user’s name.

children.title: Hearing Notice Correspondence
Hearing Notice for a Hearing Appeal
Hearing Notice for Home, Telephone, and Location Hearings
children.summary: Explains the hearing notice correspondence generated when a home, telephone, or location hearing for a hearing case is scheduled or rescheduled. The notice includes recipient name and address, plus hearing appeal details such as hearing reference number, requested and scheduled dates/times, hearing address (home or organization location), hearing case number, and issues under appeal. Telephone hearings show no address. Useful for identifying what information appears in hearing notices for different hearing types.

children.title: Hearing Notice for a Desk Based Hearing
children.summary: Explains the contents of a hearing notice for desk-based hearings. These notices are automatically generated when scheduled or rescheduled and contain no hearing instructions or continuance contact info because no one attends. The notice includes recipient details, hearing case and appeal issue details, possible aid status for continued benefits when only one appealed item exists, the hearing statement deadline, and instructions for canceling the appeal request.

Describes hearing notice correspondence for hearing appeals, focusing on what appears when hearings are scheduled or rescheduled. Covers home, telephone, location, and desk-based hearings, including recipient details, hearing reference/case numbers, requested and scheduled dates/times, appeal issues, aid status conditions, hearing statement deadlines, cancellation instructions, and hearing-specific instructions or contact info. Notes that telephone hearings omit an address, desk-based hearings have no attendance instructions or continuance contact info, and hearing notices can include restoration deadlines for missed home/phone/location hearings.

children.title: Hearing Notice for a Hearing Review
children.summary: Describes three correspondence types for hearing review events: hearing notice, hearing adjournment, and hearing attendance cancellation. The hearing notice is auto-generated when a hearing is scheduled or rescheduled and includes recipient details, hearing review identifiers, dates, appeal issues, instructions, continuance contact info, hearing statement deadline, and appeal-cancellation information; aid status appears only when there is one appealed item. Also explains adjournment notices and appellant removal notices.

Describes auto-generated hearing notice correspondence for scheduled hearing appeals and hearing review cases. Covers shared recipient and contact details, and distinguishes notice content by hearing type. For hearing appeals, includes home/telephone/location hearings with dates, times, address rules, appeal issues, aid status conditions, hearing statement deadlines, continuance/absence instructions, and appeal cancellation guidance; desk-based hearings omit attendance and continuance info but still include case details, aid status rules, deadlines, and cancellation instructions.

children.title: Hearing Adjournment Correspondence
children.summary: Explains automatically generated hearing adjournment correspondence sent when a scheduled hearing is adjourned. It notifies all hearing case participants that the adjournment was granted and includes the recipient’s name/address, the adjournment date and reason, and the name of the hearing official or reviewer who adjourned the hearing.

children.title: Hearing Attendance Cancellation Correspondence
children.summary: Explains hearing-related correspondence types. Covers hearing review notices, adjournment correspondence, and attendance cancellation correspondence. Hearing review notices are auto-generated for scheduled/rescheduled hearings and include recipient details, hearing dates, case and issue details, instructions, continuance contact info, statement deadline, and appeal cancellation instructions. Adjournment correspondence is sent when a hearing is adjourned and states recipient details, adjournment date/reason, and the official/reviewer name. Attendance cancellation correspondence is sent when an appellant is removed from a case with a scheduled hearing, telling them they need not attend.

children.title: Hearing Continuance Correspondence
children.summary: Explains hearing continuance correspondence, which is auto-generated when a scheduled hearing for an appeal or review is continued. It notifies all hearing case participants of the continuance and includes recipient name/address, continued hearing date, grant date, reason, final hearing statement deadline, cancellation-request instructions for the appellant, and the hearing official/reviewer’s name.

children.title: Representative Fee Correspondence
children.summary: Explains representative fee correspondence generated for all appeal types when a representative fee is approved or rejected. It includes the representative’s name and address, issue date, and appeal case details such as hearing reference number, appeal case reference number, appellant name, represented party name, scheduled hearing date, and representative fee. Also notes nearby correspondence types for hearing attendance cancellation and continuance.

children.title: Hearing Appeal Statement Correspondence
children.summary: Explains correspondence generated for hearing appeals when a hearing statement is recorded in time for distribution. Covers who receives hearing appeal statement correspondence, exclusions for the submitting participant and certain witness submissions, and the data shown on the letter: recipient details, issue date, appeal case details, and contact information. Also notes related witness notification correspondence for scheduled/rescheduled hearings, including when it is generated and the witness/appellant details included.

children.title: Witness Notification Correspondence
children.summary: Describes witness notification correspondence for hearing appeals. The system generates a notice from a template whenever a hearing is scheduled or rescheduled, but not for desk-based hearings. It informs voluntary witnesses that their attendance is required. The correspondence includes witness details and appellant details; if there is only one appellant, both name and address are shown, otherwise only appellant names are displayed.

children.title: Witness Summons Correspondence
children.summary: This section describes witness summons correspondence for hearing appeals. It explains that the system generates a summons for involuntary witnesses when a hearing is scheduled or rescheduled, using the witness summons template. The correspondence includes witness details, appellant details, hearing appeal details, and instructions to appear, call in, bring documents, or contact the organization for telephone hearings, with possible penalty for noncompliance. It also begins the next topic, hearing decision and order correspondence, which sends decision letters and cover letters to case participants.

children.title: Hearing Decision and Order Correspondence
children.summary: This section describes hearing-related correspondence. It covers witness summons letters, including witness and appellant details, hearing appeal details, and instructions for attending or providing a phone number and documents. It also covers hearing decision and order correspondence, which sends all case participants a decision letter and copy of the approved appeal decision. The generated cover letter includes the overall decision, and for multiple appealed items, each item’s decision. It also lists recipient details and issue date.

children.title: Court Petition Correspondence
children.summary: This section describes appeal correspondence outputs, especially court petition correspondence for judicial review appeals where the organization is the appellant. It explains that the system automatically generates and sends a court petition based on a template, and lists included details: petitioner and respondent names, appealed item reference number, decision effective date, legal reasons for reversal, and the acting user’s name. It also briefly covers related decision correspondence fields such as appeal details, decision resolutions, overall decision, further appeal date, and hearing official/reviewer name.

children.title: Hearing Transcript Correspondence
children.summary: This section describes two correspondence types in appeals processing: hearing transcript correspondence and appeal cancellation correspondence. It explains when each is generated, who receives it, and what information is included. Hearing transcript correspondence applies to judicial review appeals above the first level and includes requester details, issue date, hearing details, and the transcription itself. Appeal cancellation correspondence is automatically sent when an appeal case is canceled and includes recipient details, issue date, appeal case details, and issuing user details.

children.title: Appeal Cancellation Correspondence
children.summary: This section describes appeal cancellation correspondence, which is automatically generated whenever an appeal case is canceled and sent to all case participants. It lists the information shown on the correspondence: recipient name and address, issue date, appeal case details (hearing reference number, appeal case reference number, appellant names, and appealed items), and the issuing user’s name. The page also briefly transitions into the next topic on maintaining appeal and legal action information.

Covers appeal correspondence issued during appeals processing: receipt notices for new appeal requests, rejection letters, hearing notices for scheduled/rescheduled hearings, adjournment and continuance notices, attendance cancellation, representative fee letters, hearing statement cover letters, witness notifications and summonses, hearing decision/order letters, and court petitions for judicial review appeals. Describes when each is generated, who receives it, and the key details included such as case references, dates, issues, recipient/contact data, decision resolutions, and appeal cancellation/further appeal instructions.

children.title: 1.8 Maintaining Appeal and Legal Action Information
Search Functionality
Case Search
children.summary: This section outlines search functionality for appeals cases: users can search case information by case reference number when adding a case to an appeal. It also notes related search types for issue cases, and briefly describes items under appeal, appellants management, transcription requests, and statement submission rules. Useful for understanding how cases are found and linked to appeals, plus key hearing-related entities and constraints.

children.title: Issue Case Search
children.summary: Explains search capabilities for appeal-related data, especially issue case search. Users can search issues by case reference number, issue reference number, or the client linked to the evidence when adding an issue to an appeal case. Also briefly covers case search by case reference, items under appeal, appellant list maintenance and removal rules, transcription requests for prior hearings, and statement submission deadlines for hearing evidence.

This section describes search features for locating appeal-related records. Users can search cases by case reference number and issues by case reference number, issue reference number, or the client linked to the evidence. It also outlines related appeal concepts: items under appeal, automatic appellant list maintenance and removal limits, transcription requests for prior hearings, and statement submission deadlines for hearing evidence.

children.title: Issues under Appeal
children.summary: Explains how items are placed and managed under an appeal. Citizens choose the decision to overturn, and issues, application programs, product delivery cases, and determinations can be added as items under appeal; more items may be linked later. Also covers appeal search functions for cases and issues, automatic appellant list maintenance and removal rules, transcription requests for prior hearings, and statement submission deadlines for hearing evidence.

children.title: Appellants
children.summary: This section covers appeal search functionality, including searching for cases and issues by reference numbers or client. It explains items under appeal and how multiple items can be linked to one appeal. The “Appellants” part describes the appellants list on appeal cases, which is automatically maintained for hearing case, hearing review, and judicial review appeals; users can add or remove appellants, with limits when the case is decided or the appellant’s item is the only one on the appeal. It also briefly mentions transcription requests and statements.

children.title: Transcription Requests
children.summary: A transcription request is a request for a recorded copy of a hearing. In multi-level appeal processes, any case participant may request a transcript of the prior hearing on the case. Transcriptions are only available for hearing appeals, hearing reviews, and legal hearings.

children.title: Statements
children.summary: Explains appeal-related search functions for cases and issues, including search criteria when adding items to an appeal. Describes items under appeal and how multiple appealable items can be linked to one appeal. Covers appellant list maintenance and restrictions on removing appellants. Notes transcription requests for prior hearings in multi-level appeals. Defines statements as evidence for hearings and states they must be submitted by the deadline so participants can review them before the hearing.

children.title: Phone Contacts
children.summary: This section explains how to manage phone contacts for a hearing, including recording phone numbers for invited participants and choosing either a new or previously saved number. It also describes automatic appeal relationships between appealed items and appeal cases, including examples for multiple denied programs. Finally, it covers appeal event calendars and the system-generated events created during appeals processing, such as hearing scheduled, case created, canceled, adjourned, and completed events.

children.title: Appeal Relationships
children.summary: Explains appeal relationships and auto-created appeal events. An appeal relationship links the appealed item to the appeal case; the system creates these links automatically, including one per denied item when multiple items are appealed. Examples show links between a hearing appeal and the original case or denied benefit programs. Also describes the appeal events calendar, which records automatically generated events for appealed items, such as hearing scheduled, case created, appeal canceled, adjourned, and completed events, shown on both the original item and appeal case.

children.title: Appeal Events
children.summary: Explains appeal event handling in Cúram.
